provenance.source: IAU 2015 nominal solar radius / NASA Sun Fact Sheet

render_approximations.what: emissive sphere + point light with no distance falloff (decay 0); no limb darkening, no rotation, no corona
render_approximations.why: what matters at rung 3 is that light comes FROM the Sun's true position — Earth's terminator and the Moon's phases now follow from geometry, not from a hardcoded lamp direction
render_approximations.correction_path: physical solar rendering later; light *direction* is already correct, which is the honest part
render_approximations.expected_error: visual only — illumination direction is exact

orbit._angles_note: inclination is 0 by DEFINITION — the ecliptic is Earth's orbital plane (J2000); longitude of perihelion 102.947° carried in ω with Ω=0

provenance.source: NASA Earth Fact Sheet (mean volumetric radius; orbit: J2000 mean elements)
provenance.note: radius is the mean; Earth is an oblate spheroid (equatorial 6378.1 km, polar 6356.8 km) — sphere rendering is a knowing simplification

render_approximations.what: Keplerian two-body ellipse with FIXED mean elements — no perihelion precession, no planetary perturbations
render_approximations.expected_error: small vs true ephemeris at this rung's purposes; unquantified — honest residual, to be measured against Horizons before being trusted further

orbit._angles_note: Omega and omega are J2000 mean values and PRECESS (nodal ~18.6 yr retrograde, apsidal ~8.85 yr prograde) — held fixed here, see render_approximations

render_approximations.what: Keplerian two-body ellipse with FIXED mean elements — no apsidal/nodal precession, no perturbations (evection, variation); rendered relative to the moving Earth
render_approximations.why: rung 2 rendered the true ellipse shape and tilt; the remaining gap is that the real Moon is heavily perturbed by the Sun and its ellipse rotates
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides — real positions, not elements
render_approximations.expected_error: up to ~5,000 km positional vs true ephemeris (lunar perturbation terms) — labeled, not hidden

retired.what: orbit drawn circular at semi-major axis, in the ecliptic plane

provenance.source: NASA Mercury Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements — notably omits Mercury's famous perihelion precession (~5,600 arcsec/century, incl. the 43"/century GR term)
render_approximations.expected_error: grows secularly; fine for a labeled orrery, useless for prediction — that asymmetry is the label's whole job

provenance.source: NASA Venus Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; the nearly-circular orbit (e=0.0068) makes the residual smallest of any planet

provenance.source: NASA Mars Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; Mars's high eccentricity (0.0935) makes its aphelion/perihelion asymmetry visible in the rendered ellipse

provenance.source: NASA Jupiter Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; no moons yet (Galileans are their own future arrivals); sphere ignores the ~6.5% oblateness
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons ephemerides; oblate spheroid geometry; Galilean moons as child orbits

provenance.source: NASA Saturn Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; NO RINGS yet — Saturn without rings is a knowing omission, not an oversight; ~9.8% oblateness ignored
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons; ring geometry (measured: ~7,000–80,000 km above equator) as its own labeled arrival

provenance.source: NASA Uranus Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements; the 97.8° axial tilt is not rendered (no rotation shown at all yet)
render_approximations.correction_path: JPL Horizons; axial tilt + rotation when spin becomes visible (textures)

provenance.source: NASA Neptune Fact Sheet (volumetric mean radius; J2000 mean orbital elements)
provenance.note: the planet found by arithmetic before telescope — Le Verrier's residuals in Uranus's orbit were a labeled anomaly that got RESOLVED, the founding legend of this whole project

render_approximations.what: fixed mean elements (Neptune has completed barely one orbit since discovery — mean elements are least tested here)

provenance.source: Batygin & Brown 2021, 'The Orbit of Planet Nine' (AJ) — a≈380 AU (+140/−80), e≈0.21, i≈16°, m≈6.2 M⊕; inferred from the clustering of distant Kuiper-belt object orbits
provenance.note: EVERY number here is a posterior estimate, not a measurement — including the radius (derived from ~6 M⊕ assuming ice-giant composition; nobody has ever seen this object). The orbit shown is the distribution's peak; the real uncertainty is enormous.
provenance.falsifier: Vera Rubin Observatory (LSST) surveys the predicted swath to sufficient depth without detection AND the KBO orbital clustering is shown to be survey selection bias (as some analyses already argue) → the conjecture dies and this entry is demoted to a retired record in the provenance log. Conversely: a detection promotes it to measured. Either outcome is the system working.

render_approximations.what: single best-fit orbit drawn for what is really a broad posterior distribution; rendered ghosted + dashed so conjecture can never be mistaken for measurement
render_approximations.correction_path: on detection: real elements; on falsification: removal-by-append

provenance.note: sim day 0 puts EVERY body at periapsis simultaneously — an alignment that never happens on any real calendar date. Correction path: epoch-referenced mean anomalies, then Horizons.
random points for depth perception — NOT real star positions. Correction path: Hipparcos/Gaia bright-star catalog.
